front: Concept: first appeared in the Proslogion; argues that a 'being than which nothing greater can be conceived' must exist because existence in reality is greater than existence in the mind alone
Concept: Gaunilo's rebuttal in In Behalf of the Fool argues that a 'Lost Island' must also exist by the same reasoning, showing this argument proves too much
Philosopher: criticized the ontological argument by arguing that existence is not a real predicate, so a 'supremely perfect being' need not necessarily exist
work: Kant's Criticism (Existence Is Not a Predicate)

front: Philosopher: created a 'victorious' modal ontological argument in The Nature of Necessity using axiom S5, where considering the argument in one possible world extends it to every possible world
Philosopher: noted that Anselm actually presented two versions of the ontological argument, the first being susceptible to Kant's reply about existence
Philosopher: updated the ontological argument in The Logic of Perfection using dipolar attributes and a 'cumulative case' strategy
Philosopher: said of the ontological argument that he was certain it was wrong but could not for the life of him find a satisfying reason why
Philosopher: from the school of Isfahan; also used the ontological argument to prove God's existence; thought collected in The Four Journeys
Concept: Graham Oppy's namesake text divides this argument into six subtypes; Robert Maydole supports it with the MPA and 'temporal-contingency' arguments
